one_line_takeaway: LEG is a small (22M-304M parameter) multi-task DeBERTa guardrail that jointly predicts prompt safety and word-level explanations, trained on LLM-confirmation-bias-filtered synthetic explanation labels and a novel uncertainty-weighted focal/weak-supervision loss, matching or beating much larger LLM-based guardrails (e.g., 7-8B models) on several in-domain and out-of-domain prompt-safety benchmarks while also producing explanations shown to be faithful via a word-masking test.
key_finding: On prompt classification (unsafe-class F1, Table 1): in-domain, LEG-large reaches 87.54% on AEGIS2.0 (best of all models shown) and is comparable to but below the 8B-parameter WildGuard (88.9%) on WildGuardMix (LEG-large 87.97%) and below ToxicChat-T5-Large (82.21%) on ToxicChat0124 (LEG-large 78.58% in-domain / 69.98% best OOD). OOD, LEG-large trained on AEGIS2.0 beats the 2024 OpenAI Moderation API on ToxicChat0124 (69.98% vs 61.41%) and LEG-xs (22M) clearly outperforms the similarly sized Llama Prompt Guard 2 (22M/86M) baselines across all three OOD test sets (e.g., 84.18% vs 7.69%/8.5% on AEGIS2.0). On explanation classification (Table 2), LEG-large achieves the best in-domain scores among all evaluated methods: 79.60% (AEGIS2.0), 75.83% (WildGuardMix), 65.99% (ToxicChat0124), clearly outperforming Word/LIME/SHAP baselines (LIME/SHAP top out around 40-46% in-domain). The ablation (Table 4) shows the full joint loss beats the ablated (no weak-supervision, no focal-loss) variant in 22/36 settings overall and in 17/24 (~71%) of OOD settings specifically. The faithfulness test (Table 3) shows monotonic F1 degradation as top-1/2/3 predicted-unsafe words are masked (e.g., AEGIS2.0-trained base model drops from 86.5% full-input to 40.44% after masking top-3 words), which the authors interpret as evidence the explanations are causally faithful to the model's own decisions.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LEG (Lightweight Explainable Guardrail), a small multi-task transformer classifier that jointly performs (1) prompt-level safe/unsafe classification and (2) token-level explanation labeling (marking which words justify the safety verdict), using a shared DeBERTa-v3 encoder (xs/base/large: 22M/86M/304M params), an attention-pooling layer, and two linear heads. Three original contributions distinguish it from prior guardrails: (a) a synthetic explanation-label generation procedure that exploits LLM confirmation bias as a reliability filter -- GPT-4o-mini is queried twice with opposing framings ('why is this prompt safe?' / 'why is this prompt unsafe?'); word labels are only kept when the model's two responses agree with the true label and contradict the false framing, otherwise no word-level label is emitted for that example (Algorithm 1); (b) a novel joint multi-task loss combining cross-entropy and focal loss with two auxiliary weak-supervision signals (token-level delta_t and prompt-level delta_p 'polarization scores' computed from training-set token co-occurrence statistics), plus Kendall et al. (2018)-style learnable uncertainty weighting (sigma_1, sigma_2) between the prompt-classification and explanation-classification losses; (c) a faithfulness evaluation via word-masking perturbation showing the model's own predicted classification degrades when its own top-ranked 'unsafe' explanation words are masked from the input.
threat_model: Deployment-time input-side threat: unsafe/harmful/jailbreak prompts submitted to an LLM (hate speech, illegal-activity requests, self-harm instructions, adversarial jailbreak attempts) that a guardrail must flag before the prompt reaches the LLM. The paper does not model attacks against the guardrail itself (e.g., no adversarial-robustness or evasion analysis of LEG); it treats the guardrail purely as a classification/explainability engineering problem layered in front of an LLM, evaluated on existing prompt-safety benchmark datasets (not adaptive red-teaming against LEG).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: evaluation is conducted primarily on English-language datasets; ToxicChat0124 contains a small multilingual subset insufficient to assess multilingual performance, and no systematic multilingual/cross-domain evaluation is done, though the method is claimed to be language-agnostic in principle. Additional limitations visible in the paper's own tables (not stated explicitly by the authors as limitations but present in results): LEG underperforms domain-specialized larger baselines on ToxicChat0124 in-domain (LEG-large 78.58% vs ToxicChat-T5-Large 82.21%) and underperforms WildGuard on WildGuardMix in-domain (87.97% vs 88.9%); LEG-base specifically underperforms on ToxicChat0124 relative to other models (flagged by authors, deferred to Appendix H error analysis); the explanation classifier's F1 scores are markedly lower than the prompt classifier's across all settings (e.g., 65.99% best explanation F1 vs 87.54% best prompt F1), indicating word-level explanation remains a harder task; the ablation's ~61% win rate (22/36) for the full loss is modest overall, with the effect concentrated in OOD settings; and the synthetic explanation labels used for training/evaluating explainability come from GPT-4o-mini's own outputs, making explanation quality partially bounded by that model's judgments (the paper notes in Appendix J that this bound is not absolute but this reviewer did not verify that appendix's full text). No adversarial robustness evaluation of the guardrail itself is reported in the main text.

quantitative_results: In-domain prompt classification F1 (unsafe class): LEG-large 87.54% on AEGIS2.0 (best); LEG-large 87.97% on WildGuardMix vs WildGuard 88.9% (best); LEG-large 78.58% on ToxicChat0124 vs ToxicChat-T5-Large 82.21% (best)
OOD prompt classification: LEG-large (trained on AEGIS2.0) 69.98% on ToxicChat0124 vs OpenAI Moderation API 61.41%
OOD robustness: LEG-xs (22M) 84.18%/69.72%/56.55% across the three OOD test sets vs Llama Prompt Guard 2 22M at 7.69%/32.91%/32.13% and 86M at 8.5%/41.24%/34.16%
In-domain explanation classification F1: LEG-large 79.60% (AEGIS2.0), 75.83% (WildGuardMix), 65.99% (ToxicChat0124), all best-in-table, vs LIME-large 45.94/32.16/20.94 and SHAP-large similar range
Ablation (Table 4): full joint loss beats ablated (no delta_p, delta_t, focal loss) variant in 22/36 total settings, and 17/24 (~71%) of OOD settings
Faithfulness (Table 3): masking top-3 LEG-predicted unsafe words drops AEGIS2.0-trained base-model in-domain F1 from 86.5% (full input) to 40.44%
